title: Factors Affecting Indications
narrationScript: Every indication you read is a report on two things at once — the flaw, and the process that revealed it. The same crack can blaze, glow faintly, or stay silent depending on four factor groups. Precleaning decides whether penetrant can enter: oil, water, or residue in the flaw blocks or dilutes it, and acid or chromate residues quench fluorescent dye. The penetrant used sets the ceiling: type, method, and sensitivity level fix how bright an indication can ever be, and a degraded or contaminated tank lowers that ceiling for every part it touches. Prior processing decides whether the flaw is even open — peening, blasting, and machining smear metal over crack mouths, and coatings bridge them shut. Technique controls the rest: dwell, removal discipline, developer film, and lighting. When an indication looks weak, work the chain backward. One bad part points at cleaning or part history. Every part dim — including the known-defect panel — points at materials, equipment, or technique. Diagnose the factor before you judge the part.

sections.heading: The Indication Reports the Whole Process
sections.body:
At Level I you learned to make indications form. At Level II you read them critically — because an indication's brightness, size, and definition report on more than the flaw. They report on every process variable upstream of your eyes. The same fatigue crack can produce a bold, sharply defined line one day and a faint string of dots the next; the crack did not change — the process did.

The Level II topical outline groups the influencing factors into four: **precleaning**, the **penetrant used**, **prior processing**, and the **technique used**. Two properties make these four worth a full lesson:

- **They gate in sequence.** The flaw must be open (prior processing), its cavity empty and wettable (precleaning), the penetrant must enter, survive removal, and carry bright dye (materials and technique), and the result must be developed and seen (technique). A failure at any gate cannot be recovered at a later gate — no developer rescues a flaw the penetrant never entered.
- **They compound.** Marginal cleaning plus a slightly aggressive wash can together hide a flaw that either error alone would have left visible.

The habit this lesson builds: before you evaluate an indication — or the absence of one — ask whether the process was capable of showing you the truth.

sections.caption: Four factor groups gate the result in sequence — the weakest one sets the quality of the indication you read.

sections.heading: Precleaning: The Gate at the Flaw Mouth
sections.body:
Penetrant can only enter an opening that is **empty and wettable**. Precleaning failures attack the indication three ways:

- **Blocking the cavity.** Oils, greases, machining coolants, and water sitting inside the flaw leave no room for penetrant. The result is a weak, fragmented indication — or none at all. A flaw partly full of soil yields a partly formed indication that undersizes the discontinuity.
- **Diluting and degrading the dye.** Cleaning solvent or rinse water trapped in the flaw mixes with whatever penetrant does enter, thinning the dye into a pale, watery indication. Worse, **acid and chromate residues attack fluorescent dyes directly** — even small traces can quench fluorescence, which is why rinsing and neutralizing after chemical cleaning or etching matter as much as the cleaning itself.
- **Destroying contrast.** Residue left on the surface holds penetrant through removal and reads as background fluorescence or stain. Fine indications do not disappear — they drown.

Drying belongs to precleaning: a part that enters penetrant with water or solvent still evaporating from its flaws has its cavities already occupied. The signature of a precleaning failure is **part-specific** — one part or one lot reads dim or dirty while the rest of the line, and the known-defect process-control panel, look normal.

sections.heading: The Penetrant Used Sets the Ceiling
sections.body:
The penetrant system sets the **ceiling** on indication quality — no technique can extract more brightness than the materials can deliver.

- **Type.** Type I fluorescent penetrant viewed under UV-A in a darkened area gives far higher contrast than Type II visible red dye in white light; that is why fluorescent systems are specified for tight, fatigue-critical work. Substituting visible dye where fluorescent is specified lowers the ceiling immediately.
- **Sensitivity level.** Under AMS 2644, Type I penetrants are classified into sensitivity levels ½ through 4 — ultralow to ultrahigh; Type II carries a single level. A tight crack holds a minuscule volume of penetrant, so indication brightness rides on dye brilliance: run a Level 1 penetrant where engineering requires Level 3 and fine flaws turn dim — or drop below visibility entirely. The level is selected by the governing specification, never by the operator.
- **Method.** Method A water-washable penetrant carries built-in emulsifier, making it the most vulnerable to overwash from broad, shallow flaws. Post-emulsifiable Methods B (lipophilic) and D (hydrophilic) keep flaw-trapped penetrant water-insoluble until a separate, controlled emulsification step — which is why post-emulsifiable systems are commonly specified where the highest sensitivity must survive removal. Method C, solvent-removable, rides on wiping discipline — covered under technique.
- **Condition.** Even listed, qualified materials degrade in use: water contamination in a water-washable tank, heat from hot parts or dryer carryover, and cross-contamination between chemistries all dim fluorescence. The scheduled in-use checks — brightness, water content, removability — exist to catch exactly this.

The signature of a materials problem is **systemic**: every part reads dim, and so does the known-defect panel.

sections.heading: Prior Processing: What Happened Before Your Line
sections.body:
Everything the part went through before it reached you decides whether the flaw is still **open to the surface** — the method's first non-negotiable.

- **Mechanical smearing.** Shot peening, abrasive or vapor blasting, machining, grinding, honing, lapping, buffing, sanding, and wire brushing plastically flow surface metal. On a ductile alloy the smeared lip seals crack mouths, and a known-cracked part can process completely clean. That is why specifications call for penetrant inspection **before** peening where possible, and why **etching** — when the governing specification requires it — removes the smeared layer and reopens the flaws.
- **Coatings.** Paint, anodize, and plating bridge flaw openings, so standard practice performs PT **prior to** surface treatments unless otherwise specified. Inspecting through a coating is qualified, documented business — never a default.
- **Thermal and chemical history.** Heat-treat scale and weld oxides plug openings. Pickling and etching open flaws — but leave acids that must be neutralized and rinsed before penetrant, because trapped acid quenches fluorescent dye.
- **Previous penetrant tests.** Dried residue from an earlier test occupies the flaw, resists fresh penetrant, and its degraded dye gives little light. Worst case: residual **visible red dye quenches fluorescent penetrant**, which is why specifications generally prohibit fluorescent examination of a surface previously processed with Type II visible penetrant.

Prior-processing failures mimic precleaning failures — part-specific, often lot-specific — but no amount of recleaning fixes them. The fix lives in the part's process records and, where specified, etch.

sections.heading: Technique: The Variables in Your Hands
sections.body:
With an open, clean flaw and qualified materials, the indication now depends on discipline.

- **Application and dwell.** Dwell below the procedure minimum — commonly at least 10 minutes — leaves tight flaws partly filled and indications faint. Penetrant must stay wet through dwell; a dried film stops feeding the flaw. Standard practice keeps part and materials in a working band of roughly 40 to 125 °F: cold penetrant thickens and enters slowly, while excess heat degrades the dye.
- **Removal.** Overwashing is the classic technique kill: water too hot, too long, or at too high a pressure — typical limits sit near 50 to 100 °F with a coarse spray at no more than about 40 psi — strips penetrant out of broad, shallow flaws first. Emulsifier left beyond its qualified contact time does the same chemically. On Method C work the rule is solvent on the **wiping cloth, never flushed onto the surface** — a solvent flush dissolves penetrant straight out of the flaws.
- **Developer.** Too thin a film gives no blotting action; too heavy a coat buries fine bleed-out. Reading before development time has run — commonly at least 10 minutes — means judging indications that have not finished forming.
- **Viewing.** A formed indication still has to be seen. Fluorescent examination typically requires at least 1,000 µW/cm² of UV-A at the examination surface with ambient white light at or below about 2 footcandles, eyes dark-adapted for at least one minute, and no photochromic lenses. Visible-dye examination commonly requires at least 100 footcandles of white light. A weak lamp or a bright booth erases indications as surely as an overwash.

Technique failures can be part-specific — one rushed rinse — or systemic, like a mis-set wash station. The wash, dryer, and light settings are the first audit stops.

sections.caption: One processing line, four process histories: only the first panel reports the flaw faithfully — the other three report a process problem.
Symptom-to-cause diagnosis — typical patterns; part history and the written procedure govern.
rows: Dim, fragmented, or watery indications on one part; heavy background
Precleaning
Oil, water, or cleaner residue in the flaw; soils on the surface
Reclean, dry, and fully reprocess that part
No indications on a burnished or freshly blasted surface with a crack history
Prior processing
Peening, blasting, or machining smeared metal over flaw mouths
Check process records; etch when specified, then reprocess
No fluorescent response where a visible-dye test once marked a flaw
Prior processing
Residual Type II dye quenching fluorescence; old penetrant plugging the flaw
Review part history; disposition per the governing specification
Broad, shallow indications gone; tight cracks still show
Technique
Overwash — water time, temperature, pressure, or emulsifier overtime
Audit removal parameters against the procedure
All parts dim — and the known-defect panel dim too
Penetrant used
Degraded, contaminated, or wrong-sensitivity penetrant; weak UV-A source
Stop; verify materials and lights; reprocess parts since the last good check
Indications slow to form and faint across a cold morning shift
Technique
Part or penetrant below the working temperature band; dwell cut short
Verify temperatures and dwell time against the procedure

sections.title: Work the chain backward
sections.body: A degraded indication is a symptom — diagnose before you disposition. First question: **one part, or every part?** Part-specific dimness points at precleaning or prior processing — the part's own history. Line-wide dimness points at materials, equipment, or technique — and your proof is the **system performance check**: a known-defect standard, such as a cracked-chrome panel, processed through the line under common practice each day before production. If the panel reads weak, the process failed, every part since the last acceptable check is suspect, and the answer is reprocess. Never pass hardware on a process that could not have shown you a flaw.

figures.title: Factor chain from process input to indication quality
How each factor degrades the indication

interactive.title: Cause-and-Effect Lab: Degraded-Indication Diagnosis
interactive.intro: Four stations, four sick indications. For each one, name the causal factor — or the first move that isolates it. Work the part-specific versus systemic question before you click.

steps.prompt: A landing-gear link arrives straight from the machine shop. After full processing, the only response at a suspect fillet is a faint, broken string of dots — surrounded by smeared background fluorescence. Every other part this shift, and the morning known-defect panel, looked normal. Most likely cause?
options: The penetrant tank has degraded and lost brightness
Coolant and shop soil in and around the flaw — inadequate precleaning
The UV-A lamp is failing
Development time ran too long
steps.feedback: Part-specific dimness plus heavy background points at that part's surface condition. A degraded tank or weak lamp would have dimmed every part and the panel too. Reclean, dry, and fully reprocess the part.

steps.prompt: Overhauled compressor blades were vapor-blasted to a bright satin finish before reaching your line. Several blades with documented crack calls from teardown now process completely clean. Your first move?
options: Accept the blades — blasting evidently removed the cracks
Raise the penetrant sensitivity to Level 4 and rerun
Double the penetrant dwell time and rerun
Check whether the specification requires etch after blasting — smeared metal has likely sealed the crack mouths
steps.feedback: Blasting plastically smears ductile metal over crack mouths. No sensitivity level and no dwell time puts penetrant into a closed flaw — the remedy is the specified etch to reopen the surface, then full reprocessing.

steps.prompt: Mid-shift, every part starts reading dim under the lamp — and so does the cracked-chrome known-defect panel you run to prove the system. Which family of causes do you chase?
options: Systemic: penetrant condition, UV-A intensity, or a process setting — stop and verify before inspecting more hardware
Part-specific: a bad lot of castings hit the line
None — normal variation; keep inspecting
The panel itself is worn out; replace it and continue
steps.feedback: When the known-defect standard degrades along with production parts, the process is the suspect — materials, equipment, or technique. Inspection stops, the cause gets found, and every part since the last acceptable check is reprocessed.

steps.prompt: A welded fitting was field-checked with red visible dye last year. Today your fluorescent line shows nothing at the documented weld-toe crack, though the part processed normally everywhere else. Most likely cause?
options: The crack closed itself in service
Fluorescent penetrant cannot detect weld cracks
Residual visible dye in the flaw is quenching the fluorescent response
The developer film was too thin over the weld
steps.feedback: Trapped Type II red dye absorbs the fluorescent emission and old residue resists fresh penetrant entry — the reason specifications generally prohibit fluorescent examination of a surface previously processed with visible penetrant. Part history had the answer.

checkQuestions.question: Parts arrive at the penetrant line directly from machining, with water-based coolant still drying in pockets and recesses. If flaw cavities still hold liquid, what should a Level II expect?
options: No effect — penetrant displaces water already in the flaw
Faster indications, since liquid in the flaw speeds capillary entry
Weak, diluted, or missing indications — penetrant cannot fully enter a cavity that is already occupied
Brighter indications, because coolant fluoresces under UV-A
checkQuestions.explanation: Capillary entry needs an empty, wettable cavity. Liquid already in the flaw blocks penetrant and dilutes whatever does enter, producing pale, watery, undersized indications or none. Parts must be clean and dry before penetrant is applied.

checkQuestions.question: An engineering specification requires a Level 3 fluorescent penetrant on fatigue-critical hardware. The line is mistakenly charged with a Level 1 penetrant, and every processing step is then performed perfectly. What is the consequence?
options: Tight, small-volume flaws may produce indications too dim to see — the materials set a ceiling no technique can recover
No consequence, as long as dwell and wash are correct
Indications will be larger but fewer in number
Background fluorescence will increase sharply across the surface
checkQuestions.explanation: Sensitivity levels ½ through 4 under AMS 2644 grade a system's ability to reveal small, tight flaws. A tight crack holds a minuscule dye volume, so brightness rides on dye brilliance — a lower level than specified can leave fine flaws below visibility regardless of technique.

checkQuestions.question: A batch of ductile aluminum parts was shot peened before penetrant inspection, contrary to the planned sequence. Parts with known cracks now show nothing. Why?
options: Peening work-hardened the surface and stopped the dye from fluorescing
Peening plastically smeared metal over the crack mouths, so penetrant has no opening to enter
Peening residues chemically neutralized the developer
Compressive stress from peening pulls penetrant back out of the flaw
checkQuestions.explanation: Smearing operations — peening, blasting, buffing, some machining — flow surface metal over flaw openings and seal the door. PT is performed before peening where possible; when the specification requires it, etching removes the smeared layer and restores detectability.

checkQuestions.question: Indications from broad, shallow forging laps that showed reliably on first shift are missing on second shift, while tight crack indications still appear. Where should the investigation focus first?
options: The developer form selected for the line
The penetrant sensitivity level in the tank
The UV-A lamp intensity in the booth
Removal parameters — wash time, water temperature and pressure, or emulsifier contact time
checkQuestions.explanation: Broad, shallow flaws lose their penetrant to overremoval first; tight cracks hold theirs longest. Selective loss of shallow indications is the classic overwash signature — typical practice limits rinse water to about 50–100 °F at no more than about 40 psi.

checkQuestions.question: During a shift, all production parts and the daily known-defect panel both show noticeably dim indications. The correct response is to:
options: Extend development time until brightness recovers
Stop inspecting, verify materials and lights, and reprocess all parts run since the last acceptable system performance check
Accept the parts that showed any indications and reject the rest
Replace the known-defect panel, since it has evidently worn out
checkQuestions.explanation: A dim known-defect panel proves the process — not the parts — failed. The cause may be penetrant condition, UV-A intensity, or a process setting; until it is found and corrected, every part since the last good check is suspect and must be reprocessed.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §3.2 (factors affecting indications: precleaning, penetrant used, prior processing, technique used)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, liquid penetrant testing practice (process requirements and process control, general reference)
SAE AMS 2644, inspection material, penetrant (types, methods, sensitivity levels)
ASTM E165/E165M, liquid penetrant examination practice (general reference)
